context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |

| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the Investigation Papers assignment as a whole. Could you just explain what I’m supposed to do for these papers in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure! In short: you pick 3 out of 5 course modules (Family, Wealth, Sex, Self-Cultivation, Belonging), write one paper per module (1500–1600 words each), and each paper requires you to collect real-world data — like interviewing someone or doing an observation — then connect what you find to the course readings.

So, have you thought about which 3 modules you'd like to write on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general overview of the assignment, which is a reasonable starting point. I can summarize the key elements without doing any of the thinking for them. I'll keep it brief and then prompt them to start making their own choices.

exchanges.student: Not really, that’s part of why I’m confused. They all sound kind of similar to me, and I can’t tell what would actually count as “anthropological data” versus just me talking about stuff.
exchanges.tutor:
Good question to zero in on. Think about the difference between you sitting alone and writing your opinions versus you going out and actually doing something — talking to a real person, visiting a place, tracking your own behavior. Which of those gives you material that exists independently of what you already think?

What do you think that difference means for what counts as 'data' here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two concerns: choosing modules and understanding what counts as anthropological data. The second concern is more fundamental and will help them with the first, so I'll address that briefly with a Socratic question rather than explaining it directly. I want them to reason through what distinguishes data collection from just 'talking about stuff.'

exchanges.student: I guess data is like interviews or site visits or the app-tracking thing, but I still feel like I need the whole assignment broken down more. That didn’t totally clear it up, so maybe I’m trying a different angle: can you just walk me through one paper from start to finish in a general way?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually just nailed it — interviews, site visits, and app-tracking are exactly the kinds of data collection the assignment is built around. That's the core of it.

Walking you through a full paper would be doing your thinking for you, and that's not my role here. The better next step is to get concrete: which of the 5 modules feels most relevant or interesting to your own life right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the data collection methods, which is good progress. However, they're now asking me to walk them through a paper from start to finish, which would essentially be doing the thinking for them. I should acknowledge their correct answer about data, reinforce it briefly, and redirect them toward making their own choices rather than giving them a general walkthrough. I also want to push them to move forward concretely by picking a module.

exchanges.student: Maybe Self-Cultivation, but I’m not sure because that one also feels weirdly broad. I sort of want a bigger-picture explanation of what makes a paper in this class good, because I can’t see how the readings and the “data” are supposed to fit together.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, Self-Cultivation is a solid starting point. On your bigger question: think about what readings do in any academic paper — they give you a framework or lens. So what do you think it would look like to use a course reading as a *lens* to interpret something you actually observed or heard?

Also, which of the two Self-Cultivation options appeals to you more — the app-tracking or the hobby interview?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around a general anxiety about the assignment rather than diving in. They've now picked a tentative module (Self-Cultivation), which is progress. Their question about how readings and data fit together is valid but risks becoming another way to delay starting. I should briefly address the readings/data question with a Socratic nudge, then anchor them to their module choice to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know what “lens” means in practice here. I’m tempted to say the hobby interview, but could you maybe just give me a sample structure or rough outline, because I keep feeling like I’m missing the overall shape.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide an outline — that's for you to develop. But let's make 'lens' concrete: imagine you interview a friend about their hobby and they tell you it gives them a sense of community. Now imagine a course reading talks about how self-cultivation practices connect people to larger social groups. How might that reading help you *say something more* about what your friend told you, beyond just reporting it?

What hobby are you thinking of interviewing someone about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for structural scaffolding and explanations rather than engaging with the actual work. I shouldn't provide an outline or sample structure — that would be doing the intellectual work for them. I should address the 'lens' confusion with a concrete Socratic question tied to their chosen option, and gently push back on the outline request. They've chosen the hobby interview, which is progress — I should lock that in and move forward.

exchanges.student: Maybe my roommate’s rock climbing, but I’m still pretty stuck and that didn’t really work for me. Let me try a different angle: I don’t need the full paper, I just want to know what sections people usually include so I’m not guessing blindly.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear that you're feeling stuck, but providing a structure is something I won't do — that's part of the intellectual work that needs to be yours.

You have a topic: your roommate's rock climbing. The assignment gives you a list of specific questions to explore. Have you read through the Option 2 prompt carefully? Pick one question from it that feels like a natural place to start a conversation with your roommate.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for structural guidance (outline/sections) which I should not provide. This is now a pattern — they keep rephrasing the same request. I need to set a clear boundary here and redirect them toward actual engagement with their chosen topic. Rock climbing is a good concrete starting point. I should be direct about not providing a structure and push them toward the actual work.

exchanges.student: I read it, but all the questions blur together for me. Could you at least say, in broad terms, what the beginning, middle, and end of a paper like this are supposed to do?
exchanges.tutor:
This is the third time you've asked for a structure in different words, and my answer stays the same — I won't provide it.

Here's what I'd suggest: stop trying to see the whole shape before you start, and just pick ONE question from the prompt to ask your roommate. Which one feels most interesting to you personally?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for an outline or structure multiple times in different ways. This is a redundant pattern and I need to firmly but kindly decline and redirect. I should not soften my boundary here. Instead, I should push them toward concrete action — they have a topic, they have interview questions in the prompt, they just need to start. I'll be direct and move them forward.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe “how important is the hobby to their sense of self?” I’m still not confident, though, and that approach hasn’t helped much, so I’m trying another angle: can you explain what would make my paper analytical instead of just a summary?
exchanges.tutor:
Good pick. Now, think about this: if your roommate says 'rock climbing is super important to who I am,' and you just write that down and move on — what's missing?

What would you need to *do* with that answer to go beyond just reporting it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress — they've picked a module, an option, a topic, and now a specific interview question. That's good forward momentum. Their question about analytical vs. summary writing is legitimate and different from the structural questions they've been asking. I should briefly engage with it Socratically, connecting it back to the 'lens' concept and their chosen question, without explaining it fully for them.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d need to explain why it matters, or connect it to a bigger idea from class, but I’m still fuzzy on what that looks like on the page. Could you just give me a very short example of the difference between summary and analysis for something like this?
exchanges.tutor:
You just described analysis correctly — connecting what you heard to a bigger idea. That's it.

So try applying it right now: what bigger idea from the Self-Cultivation readings might connect to your roommate's sense of identity through rock climbing? What readings have you done for that module?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown genuine progress in understanding — they correctly identified that analysis means connecting to bigger ideas. This is actually a good answer. Rather than giving an example (which would be doing the work for them), I should affirm their insight and push them to apply it themselves to their specific case. They're close to being able to do this on their own.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the readings, which is part of the problem. I think I need to back up a lot more than that—could you just explain the assignment in a broad, general way without making me choose details yet?
exchanges.tutor:
We're not going back to the beginning — you've actually made a lot of progress. You have a module, an option, a topic, a starting interview question, and you correctly explained what analysis means. That's a solid foundation.

The gap you're naming is the readings, and that's something I can't fix for you — you need to go back and read the Self-Cultivation materials. Once you've done that, come back and we can work with what you find there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking to go back to the very beginning, which is a clear regression and avoidance pattern. We've made significant concrete progress — they have a module, option, topic, interview question, and even articulated what analysis means. Going back to a broad explanation would undo all of that. I need to firmly redirect them and point out the progress they've already made. The real issue seems to be that they haven't done the readings, which I should name directly.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the tutor notes this is the 'third time you've asked for a structure' but does not attempt diagnostic questioning to understand *why* the student keeps returning to this request — whether it's anxiety, a genuine conceptual gap, or something else. The tutor simply repeats the refusal and redirects without probing the underlying confusion more carefully.

The student asks for a structure or outline in Turns 5, 6, 7, and 10, and the tutor's response is essentially the same refusal each time without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing progress, or switching strategy to break the cycle. By Turn 7 the tutor acknowledges the repetition but still does not shift to a substantively different approach (e.g., summarizing what the student already knows, offering a reframe of the task, or addressing the anxiety driving the loop).

grade.overview: The tutor does well overall: it avoids doing the student's work, maintains a Socratic approach, keeps responses concise, and correctly identifies the student's actual gap (not having read the module materials) by the end. The main weaknesses are a failure to break out of a repetitive loop when the student keeps requesting structure, and a missed opportunity to diagnose *why* the student is stuck before simply repeating the refusal.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never provides outlines, sample structures, or submission-ready content, consistently redirecting the student to do their own thinking. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: when the student spirals on the structure request across multiple turns, the tutor does not pause to ask diagnostic questions about the root of the confusion (anxiety? unfamiliarity with academic writing? not having done the readings?) before responding. 2.1 loses all 4 points: the student asks for a structure or outline in at least four turns (5, 6, 7, 10), and the tutor's response is functionally identical each time — a refusal plus a redirect to a narrow next step — without consolidating progress, reframing the task, or switching to a meaningfully different strategy to break the cycle. 1.3, 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all clean with no evidence of deductible behavior.
